title: Stacking Recurrent Layers
concept: Deep Recurrent Networks stack multiple RNN/LSTM layers on top of each other, where the hidden states of layer 1 become the inputs to layer 2.

exercises.prompt: Before we stack recurrent layers, let's review: What type of data are recurrent networks (RNNs) best at processing?

options.text: Sequential data like text or time-series

exercises.explain: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are designed to process sequential data, like text or audio, because they keep a memory of past steps.

cards.title: Going Deeper with Memory
cards.body: We know that a single recurrent layer has a memory of the past. But what if we want our AI to understand complex, layered ideas like the overarching plot of a novel? Just like stacking building blocks, we can stack recurrent layers to build deeper, smarter AI minds!

cards.title: What is Stacking Recurrent Layers?
cards.body: A Deep Recurrent Network stacks multiple recurrent layers (like RNNs, LSTMs, or GRUs) on top of each other. Instead of sending the output directly to the final answer, the first layer passes its step-by-step memory (hidden states) upward to serve as the input sequence for the next layer.

cards.title: The Translation Assembly Line
cards.body: Think of a single RNN layer like a solo translator trying to translate a book page by page. Stacking recurrent layers is like an assembly line of translators: the first translator decodes the raw text into basic words, and then passes their work to a second, more experienced translator who understands the deeper, overall plot of the story.

cards.title: Deep Reading in Action
cards.body:
When predicting the next word in a complex paragraph:
• Layer 1 reads the raw words and detects simple patterns, like grammar and punctuation.
• Layer 2 reads Layer 1's patterns to track the mood of the text.
• Layer 3 reads Layer 2's mood to predict the exact next word, such as choosing a scary word for a horror story!

cards.title: Myth: More Layers is Always Better
cards.body: It is easy to think that adding 100 layers will make your network infinitely smarter. In reality, stacking too many recurrent layers makes the model very slow, hard to train, and prone to forgetting early information. Two or three layers are usually plenty for most tasks!

cards.title: Single vs. Stacked RNNs
cards.body: Compare a simple single-layer recurrent network with a deep stacked recurrent network.

right.text: Layer 1 processes raw input sequence, passes sequences upward, and Layer 2 processes those to make the final prediction. Great for complex language structure.

left.text: Reads raw input sequence and immediately outputs the prediction. Great for simple tasks.

exercises.prompt: How does the first recurrent layer pass information to the second recurrent layer above it?

options.text: It only sends the final summary of the sequence.
It passes its step-by-step memory at every point in time.
It bypasses the second layer completely.
It stops processing and hands over control.

exercises.explain: In stacked recurrent layers, the lower layer must output its hidden states at every time step (a full sequence) so the upper layer has sequential data to process.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Stacking recurrent layers helps the network learn more complex and hierarchical patterns than a single layer can.
exercises.explain: Stacked recurrent networks are much better at learning complex hierarchies and abstract patterns than a single recurrent layer.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how information flows upward in a stacked network.
exercises.sentence: The hidden states output by the first layer become the sequential [blank] for the second layer.
exercises.explain: In a stacked network, the outputs of the lower layer become the inputs for the layer directly above it.

exercises.prompt: Two developers are designing a 2-layer stacked RNN. Which model is built correctly?

options.text: AI Model A: Layer 1 outputs a single summary vector -> Layer 2 reads this single vector.
AI Model B: Layer 1 outputs a sequence of states -> Layer 2 reads this sequence of states.
AI Model C: Both Layer 1 and Layer 2 read the raw input text at the exact same time.

exercises.explain: AI Model B is correct because the intermediate layer (Layer 1) must output a sequence to feed Layer 2. AI Model A fails because Layer 1 only outputs a single vector, meaning Layer 2 gets no sequence to read.

exercises.prompt: You are building a deep RNN with three stacked LSTM layers. In Keras or PyTorch, how must you configure the 'return_sequences' or sequence output settings for the first two layers so the network works correctly?

options.text: All layers except the first layer must set it to False.
All layers before the final layer must set it to True.
Only the final layer must set it to True.
Every layer in the entire network must set it to False.

exercises.explain: When stacking recurrent layers, intermediate layers must output their hidden state at every single step (return_sequences=True) so the next layer has a sequence of inputs to read. Only the final layer can choose to output just the last step's summary.
